title: Bowie

jokes.text: Bret refuses roll call by saying 'No' and 'I'm not here' while visibly sitting in the meeting
jokes.setup: Murray calls roll call as if it's a serious bureaucratic procedure
jokes.punchline: Bret says 'No' and 'I'm not here' — refusing to participate in the most basic formality while obviously present
jokes.explanation: Pure deadpan from Bret — the absurdity of denying your own presence while present is perfectly Conchords. Simple, economical, character-specific (Bret's passive resistance to Murray's bureaucracy).

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Bret?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: What do you mean no?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I'm not here.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You're not here?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Where are you?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No, I was just joking.

jokes.text: Murray reveals he has held band meetings without the band present and marked them 'absent'
jokes.setup: Murray defends the roll call because there have been times the band wasn't there
jokes.punchline: He confirms he just carried on with the meeting without them — the meeting has no purpose without the band, making its continuation meaningless bureaucracy
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character comedy. Murray's compulsive adherence to procedure over function is the core joke of his entire character — holding a band meeting without the band perfectly encapsulates his bureaucratic absurdity. High on character integration because only Murray would do this and find it perfectly reasonable. The follow-up 'they didn't go very well' is a beautiful understated add-on.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Murray explaining he's carried on band meetings without the band members being there

jokes.text: The band photo turns out to be of Gemma's 21st birthday with neither Bret nor Jemaine in it, and Murray has circled their cousins
jokes.setup: Murray presents the band's official photo as inadequate — he's had to circle where they are
jokes.punchline: The photo is from someone's 21st birthday party, neither band member is in it, and Murray has circled the wrong people entirely
jokes.explanation: Escalating reveal structure — first we learn he had to circle them, then that neither is in it, then that he circled the wrong people, then that the photographer was Bret himself. Each beat lands. Visual comedy enhanced by the audience actually seeing the photo.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: We're at a band meeting. We haven't been here?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, twice. I mean...

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: When?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well, it doesn't matter when. They didn't go very well.

jokes.text: 'I think I took this photo.' / 'Yeah, I took it.' — Bret realizes neither band member is in their own promo photo because Bret was the photographer
jokes.setup: Murray asks where Bret and Jemaine are in the photo
jokes.punchline: They gradually realize Bret took the photo — so neither is in it
jokes.explanation: The slow dawning realization delivered with complete deadpan works well. The gradual 'I think... yeah' acknowledgment is funnier than an instant reveal would be.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: But I've had to circle where you two are.

jokes.text: 'It's got a good energy.' Murray responds to being told the photo has no band members in it
jokes.setup: The photo has been revealed to contain neither band member
jokes.punchline: Murray's defense is that it has 'a good energy' — a PR/management cliché applied to a completely useless band photo
jokes.explanation: Murray's stubborn defense of a clearly useless photo using hollow industry language is a perfect three-word gag. His follow-up 'I know, I tell people that' confirms he's been actively using this terrible photo.

dialogue.line: Photo of Gemma's 21st birthday with Murray circling random cousins thinking they're the band

jokes.text: The second photo: Murray has superimposed Bret's head over Jemaine's ex-girlfriend Clea's head on a personal photo — and Murray made it himself
jokes.setup: Murray presents the only other available band photo as an alternative
jokes.punchline: It's Jemaine's ex-girlfriend's photo with Bret's head glued over hers — and Murray created it himself and considers it a viable promo option
jokes.explanation: A genuinely original absurdist gag — the photo is bizarre on multiple levels: stolen from Jemaine's personal collection, involves his ex-girlfriend, and features a crude head-swap. Murray presenting it as a legitimate option reveals the depths of his incompetence. 'I loved that photo' from Jemaine adds a layer of pathos. Scores high because the absurdity is earned and specific.

dialogue.line: Photo of Jemaine with ex-girlfriend Clea but with Bret's head superimposed

jokes.text: 'This one here, you're not in. And this one here you're in, but you shouldn't be.'
jokes.setup: Murray summarizing their two photo options
jokes.punchline: The perfectly parallel construction highlights the absurdity of both situations with deadpan symmetry
jokes.explanation: One of the best-structured lines in the episode. The parallel construction — 'not in' vs 'shouldn't be' — is elegant, economical, and perfectly captures both photo failures in one breath. High structure and economy scores are justified; this is the kind of line a good comedy writer is proud of.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I made it. I made this one.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You can't take my personal photos and stick other people's heads over... I loved that photo.

jokes.text: Murray calls the photo shoot a 'photo shoot' with a dramatic pause as if it's an exciting revelation
jokes.setup: Murray builds to announcing his solution
jokes.punchline: After the buildup of '...what's called a...' the answer is just the obvious and mundane 'photo shoot'
jokes.explanation: Mild anti-climax joke — Murray dramatizes saying the obvious. Works as character comedy (Murray's self-importance) but not particularly original or sharp.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: 'this one here, you're not in. And this one here you're in, but you shouldn't be'

jokes.text: The photo shoot is happening in the New Zealand consulate hallway with a passport camera that only Greg is allowed to touch — even Murray can't touch it
jokes.setup: Murray conducts the 'professional' photo shoot
jokes.punchline: It's a government passport camera, Greg is the designated toucher, and this is the extent of their photographic resources
jokes.explanation: The detail that Murray himself is not permitted to touch the camera — and defers to Greg — is a lovely absurdist touch. Establishes that the entire 'photo shoot' is a charmingly shambolic affair run on government property with borrowed equipment.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: So those are you options. This one here, you're not in. And this one here you're in, but you shouldn't be.

jokes.text: Murray insists musicians smile and put their hands in the air, confusing this with what gymnasts do
jokes.setup: Jemaine adopts the 'cool depressed' band look; Murray wants smiles and raised arms
jokes.punchline: Jemaine and Bret correct Murray — that's what gymnasts do, not musicians. Murray asks Greg for confirmation and accepts this.
jokes.explanation: Murray's cluelessness about rock aesthetics is a solid character beat. The escalation of asking Greg — a third-party office worker — to adjudicate between musicians and gymnasts is a nice absurdist flourish.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You can't look through that. It's New Zealand government property.

jokes.text: 'Jemaine, you look like an ogre that works in the library.'
jokes.setup: Murray is trying to explain why the photo sizing looks off — Bret appears small
jokes.punchline: Rather than technical feedback, Murray delivers this specific, absurdly precise insult about Jemaine's appearance
jokes.explanation: A highly quotable, precision-crafted insult. The specificity of 'an ogre that works in the library' elevates this above a generic size joke — the juxtaposition of menacing mythological creature with the mundane professional setting of a library is genuinely funny and imaginative. Economy is near-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: 'Who wants to hear a sad band?'

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: 'This is the cool look for a band.'

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: 'But what about your hands in the air and smile?'

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: 'That's gymnasts, Murray.'

jokes.text: Bret trying to fill conversational silence by bringing up penguins, then abandoning the subject — 'Bret, I'm kind of doing all the work in this conversation.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine can tell something is wrong with Bret and tries to engage him
jokes.punchline: Bret floats the non-sequitur topic of penguins then drops it entirely, leaving Jemaine doing all conversational labor
jokes.explanation: The penguin non-sequitur ('Sometimes I'm glad we don't live in New Zealand anymore. 'Cause of all those penguins.') is perfectly absurdist and the meta-comment on the failed conversation is self-aware and funny. Captures the particular social texture of two people who have run out of small talk.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: That's gymnasts, Murray.

jokes.text: Jemaine repeatedly suggests Bret is bulimic when Bret says he just feels like he needs to eat more
jokes.setup: Bret says he has body image issues and just needs compliments and food
jokes.punchline: Jemaine keeps re-diagnosing this as bulimia and citing a drunken birthday party bathroom incident as evidence
jokes.explanation: The running insistence on bulimia — and particularly 'being a little bit bulimic' as if it's a spectrum — plus citing the birthday party bathroom as medical evidence is solid character comedy. Jemaine's clinical confidence about something he's clearly wrong about is characteristic of the show's deadpan.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: you look like an ogre that works in the library

jokes.text: Bret asks for compliments; Jemaine refuses because 'it'd be weird,' then immediately agrees it'd be weird
jokes.setup: Bret needs reassurance and asks Jemaine directly for compliments
jokes.punchline: Jemaine refuses on the grounds of social weirdness; Bret immediately agrees and gives up; Jemaine doesn't even try
jokes.explanation: The emotional texture of male friendship — unable to give or receive direct affirmation — is the whole engine of the upcoming song. The setup here is efficient and the mutual awkwardness is true to the characters.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Sometimes I'm glad that we don't live in New Zealand anymore. 'Cause of all those penguins.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Remember how there'd be penguins?

jokes.text: Jemaine appears as 1972 David Bowie from the Ziggy Stardust tour in Bret's dream
jokes.setup: Bret goes to sleep dejected; we cut to a dream sequence
jokes.punchline: Jemaine in full Ziggy Stardust costume — makeup, lightning bolt — appears as the visitation of David Bowie to boost Bret's confidence
jokes.explanation: The central high-concept gag of the episode — Jemaine playing David Bowie as a dream-guide for Bret's body image issues is an inspired premise. The show's form (Jemaine IS a tall, dark, vaguely exotic presence) makes the casting commentary meta and funny. High character integration because only in this specific show would this specific casting choice make sense.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah... no.

jokes.text: 'Wow, you look a lot like Jemaine.' / 'No, I'm David Bowie.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine appears in full Bowie costume in Bret's dream
jokes.punchline: Bret points out the obvious resemblance; Jemaine flatly denies it
jokes.explanation: The flat denial of the obvious is very New Zealand deadpan. The joke is economical and the 'no, I'm David Bowie' is delivered with total sincerity. Classic straight-faced absurdism.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I feel small. I need to eat more.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Are you bulimic?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No, I just need to eat more food.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Are you bulimic?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Are you bulimic?

jokes.text: Bret interrupts David Bowie's dream-vision to describe his own previous guinea pig and giraffe dreams at length
jokes.setup: Jemaine-as-Bowie asks if he's freaking Bret out with the dream
jokes.punchline: Rather than acknowledge the supernatural event, Bret veers into describing his own more interesting past dreams — 'I had one where I was a guinea pig, but with my face'
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'a guinea pig but with my face' is a great absurdist detail. Bret's total lack of appropriate awe at David Bowie's supernatural visitation — instead trying to one-up the encounter with his own dream inventory — is perfect character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I heard Bret being a little bit bulimic in the bathroom after his mom's birthday

jokes.text: David Bowie's advice: 'Get an eye patch, man.' / 'I'm sorry?' / 'Get an eye patch.'
jokes.setup: Bowie has spoken about breaking through false barriers; Bret expects meaningful wisdom
jokes.punchline: The grand cosmic advice is simply to wear an eye patch
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection — after pseudo-profound mystical language ('the plastic pantomime'), the advice bottoms out into something completely mundane and practical. The repetition ('I'm sorry?' / 'Get an eye patch.') delivered without any justification is excellent deadpan.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Can you just tell me I'm good at something?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No, it'd be weird.

jokes.text: '# Wear the eye patch, Bret / Wear the funky funky eye patch. #' — Bowie's two-line dream song
jokes.setup: Bowie has delivered his wisdom; the dream should end
jokes.punchline: Instead, he delivers a tiny impromptu two-line song to reinforce the eye patch advice before departing
jokes.explanation: Micro-song as punchline is a FOTC specialty and this two-liner is executed perfectly. The Bowie-esque 'funky funky' is a great pastiche detail. The brevity is the joke — it's a full musical commitment to a completely trivial piece of advice.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: It's 1972 David Bowie from the Ziggy Stardust tour

jokes.text: 'Watch out for the table!' / 'Sorry about the table, Bret.' — Bowie exits by walking into furniture
jokes.setup: Bowie makes a mystical exit from the dream
jokes.punchline: He immediately walks into a table; Bret warns him too late; Bowie apologizes for the table
jokes.explanation: The deflation of the mystical dream visitation by a mundane collision is perfectly timed. The polite 'Sorry about the table, Bret' from someone playing the rock god David Bowie is a brilliant anticlimax. Seeds the later payoff when the eye patch causes identical depth-perception problems for both Bret and real Bowie.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Wow, you look a lot like Jemaine

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No, I'm David Bowie

jokes.text: Murray on the phone: 'No, he's not a midget. He's just... he's thin.'
jokes.setup: Murray is apparently on the phone talking about the photo of the band
jokes.punchline: Someone on the other end has mistaken Bret's small appearance in the photo for dwarfism; Murray's correction focuses entirely on Bret's thinness rather than challenging the framing
jokes.explanation: A good in medias res gag — we drop into this phone call without context and Murray's matter-of-fact explanation ('there's the full range') is deadpan gold. Continues the episode's running thread of Bret's body image via external perception.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Bret's guinea pig and giraffe dream descriptions

jokes.text: Bret arrives wearing an eye patch to the meeting about the magazine feature
jokes.setup: Dream Bowie advised wearing the eye patch; Bret has taken this seriously
jokes.punchline: Bret arrives wearing it as his 'new look' in a mundane daytime context
jokes.explanation: The callback to the dream advice playing out in reality. The casual acceptance of the eye patch as a 'new look' while the magazine feature reveals they've already made it into print is a good layered moment.

dialogue.speaker: Bowie
dialogue.line: Yeah, okay. Listen, Bret, I don't have much time, man.

jokes.text: Murray outs Bret as bulimic in the middle of the pitch meeting setup — 'Bret's bulimic.'
jokes.setup: Murray is giving a pep talk before their big meeting
jokes.punchline: He casually mentions Bret's 'bulimia' as if it's a relevant action item, despite Bret's repeated denials
jokes.explanation: The running bulimia gag escalates — now Murray has absorbed the misdiagnosis and is treating it as official band business, including it in the pre-meeting briefing. 'He's not now, he's fine now' / 'It's cleared up' while Bret protests is good cringe.

dialogue.speaker: Bowie
dialogue.line: Get an eye patch, man

jokes.text: 'Is that a music term, "Rock it out"?' / 'Yeah, that's one.'
jokes.setup: Murray says 'let's rock it out' as a motivational close to the meeting
jokes.punchline: Jemaine sincerely asks if it's a music industry term; Murray confirms it is, as if he's citing a technical term
jokes.explanation: A brief but well-executed exchange. Jemaine's genuine curiosity about whether a common phrase is technical jargon — and Murray's confident affirmation — is typical of the show's dry absurdism about music-industry naivety.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: two different sizes there. There's the full range.

jokes.text: Jemaine asks Mel if she prefers him over Bret — and she says yes with no hesitation — while her husband Doug sits right there
jokes.setup: Jemaine wants to enlist Mel to compliment Bret and needs to confirm she prefers him first
jokes.punchline: Mel confirms her preference for Jemaine immediately and enthusiastically, not remotely troubled by Doug's presence
jokes.explanation: Mel's obsession with the band — to the exclusion of basic marital propriety — is the core of her character. The 'Happy anniversary, by the way' exchange in the middle of her confirming she prefers another man is perfect cringe. Doug's passive acceptance is equally funny.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No no, he's not a midget. He's just... he's thin.

jokes.text: Jemaine refuses to compliment Bret himself because 'it might be gay' — then the conversation with Mel escalates into graphic descriptions of gay sex
jokes.setup: Jemaine says complimenting Bret would be weird / might be gay
jokes.punchline: Mel corrects him — complimenting a friend isn't gay — but then proceeds to elaborate increasingly specifically on what WOULD be gay: 'two men touching each other physically and emotionally, erotically caressing each other on the hood of a car, in the back of a movie theater'
jokes.explanation: The escalation from Jemaine's mild homophobia to Mel's increasingly specific and enthusiastic descriptions of gay encounters is perfectly structured. Jemaine's attempts to wrap up the conversation ('Hey, thanks for your help anyway') while Mel continues ('...in the back of a movie theater') is excellent cringe comedy. The joke is also on Jemaine — his nervousness about a simple compliment has led to this. High scores across structure, character integration, and earned-vs-cheap.

dialogue.line: Greeting cards with hidden music technology - Murray's confused explanations

jokes.text: Mel reveals she's the junior professor of psychology; Doug was the senior professor but was fired and 'just hangs around here'
jokes.setup: Jemaine asks what they do at the university to close the awkward conversation
jokes.punchline: Doug was the senior psychology professor but was fired — and now just haunts the university campus
jokes.explanation: 'And now I just hang around here' delivered flatly by Doug is a beautifully sad and funny character beat. The passive acceptance of his demotion, his wife's embarrassing behavior, and his general life circumstances is played completely straight. The psychology background makes the couple's dynamic additionally ironic.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It's a company that sells greeting cards.
Greeting cards?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: But you know those ones that open up and they have a tune in them?

jokes.text: Jemaine attempts compliments: 'Your beard is good.' / 'It's just a compliment for you... your beard.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine has decided to give Bret compliments after all
jokes.punchline: The best he can manage is 'your beard is good' — and he over-explains it as if clarifying the social function of what he's doing
jokes.explanation: The follow-up self-explanation ('just a compliment about your beard being good') reveals Jemaine's profound discomfort with male affirmation. The second compliment — 'you're good at finding shortcuts around this part of town' — is an equally brilliant choice: deeply impersonal, geographically specific, and completely unrelated to Bret's self-esteem issues.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I think it might be gay to compliment him myself

jokes.text: 'You're good at finding shortcuts around this part of town.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine is trying to give Bret compliments to improve his self-esteem
jokes.punchline: The compliment offered is about Bret's navigational skills in their local neighborhood — completely irrelevant to body image or personal worth
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The extraordinary specificity and irrelevance of this compliment — geographic, hyper-local, banal — perfectly captures both Jemaine's inability to offer genuine affirmation AND his fundamental affection for Bret. It's funnier than any insult could be. Near-perfect economy. Very quotable. This is what a high-craft joke looks like in low-key comedy: unexpected specificity delivering genuine character truth.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Erotically caressing each other on the hood of a car... in the back of a movie theater

jokes.text: 'Would it be gay to write you a song to cheer you up?' / 'No.'
jokes.setup: Having offered two inadequate compliments, Jemaine escalates to a song
jokes.punchline: He frames writing a song for his bandmate as a potential gay act and seeks prior consent; Bret's flat 'No' gives him the green light
jokes.explanation: Efficient bridge to the song. The framing of a musical gesture as potentially gay — while also being fundamentally what they do professionally — is a nice irony. The deadpan exchange captures the show's voice perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Doug
dialogue.line: Doug was fired as senior professor but still hangs around the university

jokes.text: 'Bret, You Got It Going On' — the full song, particularly the 'not in a gay way' verse and the revelation that Jemaine put a wig on Bret while he slept on tour
jokes.setup: Jemaine writes a song to boost Bret's confidence about his looks and romantic prospects
jokes.punchline: The song escalates from genuine-ish compliments ('sure you're weedy and kinda shy') through an extended 'not in a gay way' disclaimer verse, to the revelation that Jemaine put a wig on Bret while he slept and 'just lay there and spooned you' on tour
jokes.explanation: The centerpiece of the episode and one of the show's best songs. It earns its high score through structural perfection: the song begins as a heartfelt compliment, immediately pivots into defensive heterosexual disclaimers ('not in a gay way, just in a hey, man, I wanted to say'), continues to escalate those disclaimers ('I don't want to bum you'), and then — in a jaw-dropping reveal — admits Jemaine put a wig on Bret and spooned him while he slept. The song's purpose (to make Bret feel better) is completely subverted by what it reveals. This is character-integrated comedy at its finest: the song only works coming from Jemaine, it reveals something true about his character (deep affection masked by denial), and it functions as comedic escalation, relationship comedy, and surreal musical parody simultaneously. 'If one of us was lucky enough to be born a lady' / 'I put a wig on you when you were sleeping / I just lay there and spooned you' is television comedy writing at a very high level.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Your beard is good

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You're good at finding shortcuts

jokes.text: Bret asks to look at the lyrics after the song
jokes.setup: Jemaine has just performed an intimate, revealing song to Bret
jokes.punchline: Bret's first response is to request the written lyrics — treating it as a document to be reviewed, not a personal confession
jokes.explanation: Perfect beat after the song — the shift from emotional revelation to bureaucratic review is classically Conchords. Pure economy: four words, big laugh. The deadpan request to audit the song that just emotionally exposed Jemaine is very funny.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Your beard is good. It's just a compliment for you... your beard. Just a compliment about your beard being good.

jokes.text: Jemaine denies having said anything about a wig despite it being in the song he literally just performed
jokes.setup: Bret confronts Jemaine about the wig lyric
jokes.punchline: Jemaine insists he didn't say anything like that — despite having just sung it — and pivots to 'I think sometimes you hear what you want to hear'
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's immediate gaslighting of Bret about lyrics he literally just sang is a beautiful character beat. 'I think sometimes you hear what you want to hear' applied to a song he composed is surreal and very funny. The follow-up — 'It wouldn't be gay to put a wig on a man and pretend they're a woman. How could that be gay if you were pretending they're a woman?' — delivered as a genuine philosophical defense is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Bret, you got it going on

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You're like a beautiful woman

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You're like a beautiful woman

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: And I want to make love to you

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: But it's not gay

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I'm just saying you're beautiful

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Like a man can say to another man

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: He's beautiful

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: And it's not weird

jokes.text: 'Not that I did it.' — Jemaine's brief, isolated disclaimer after philosophically defending the non-gayness of wig-spooning
jokes.setup: Jemaine has just argued that putting a wig on a man and pretending he's a woman isn't gay
jokes.punchline: A beat of silence, then: 'Not that I did it.'
jokes.explanation: The funniest single line in the episode. After a lengthy and earnest philosophical defense of an act he just described in a song, Jemaine delivers this four-word non-denial denial after a beat of silence. Perfect comedic timing, perfect character — his complete inability to actually deny something he already confessed to is pure Jemaine. Economy score of 10 is warranted: there is literally not a single word that could be added or removed. This is what a 9+ joke looks like.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Not in a gay way, just in a hey, man, I wanted to say / That you're looking okay, man, why can't a heterosexual guy / Tell a heterosexual guy that he thinks his booty is fly?

jokes.text: Second dream: 1980 Bowie from 'Ashes to Ashes' video appears — Jemaine in a different Bowie-era costume
jokes.setup: Bret has a second dream visitation from Bowie
jokes.punchline: This time Bowie identifies himself by specific era and music video — played with complete sincerity — establishing the running structure of the episode
jokes.explanation: The running structure of Jemaine playing different Bowie eras — each one more specific — is a solid running gag. The humor compounds with each visit.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I put a wig on you when you were sleeping / I put a wig on you... you-hoo-hoo-hoo / And I just lay there and spooned you

jokes.text: 'Actually, come to think of it, the same thing happened to me when I wore an eye patch. It was always, "Bowie's bumped into a door."'
jokes.setup: Bowie is sympathizing about Bret's depth perception problems
jokes.punchline: Bowie reveals the exact same thing happened to him — undermining any authority the advice had and retroactively explaining his own table collision in the previous dream
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed two-episode callback that retroactively recontextualizes Bowie's table collision as chronic eye-patch-related door bumping. The structure is excellent: the advice given turns out to be advice Bowie couldn't follow himself. The reported speech ('Bowie's bumped into a door') with its third-person formulation is a very funny detail — as if it was notable enough to become a news item.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I think sometimes you hear what you want to hear

jokes.text: Bret asks Bowie if a friend putting a wig on you while lonely and pretending you're a woman is gay
jokes.setup: Bret is seeking external validation about Jemaine's wig behavior
jokes.punchline: He's consulting his dream David Bowie for a ruling on whether his bandmate's wig-spooning constitutes a gay act
jokes.explanation: The callback structure is elegantly handled — Bret uses his access to a dream deity to resolve a real dispute from waking life. Bowie's long pause before ruling 'No, that's not gay. Are you sure? Totally fine.' has the weight of an official judgment. The absurdity of this being the philosophical question brought to David Bowie is very funny.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: It wouldn't be gay to put a wig on a man and pretend they're a woman. How could that be gay if you were pretending they're a woman?

jokes.text: Bowie's second piece of advice: 'It doesn't hurt to do something absolutely outrageous.' / 'Like what?' / 'Well, you'll know what to do, Bret. And you'll know exactly when the time is right.'
jokes.setup: Bowie delivers profound-sounding advice about being outrageous
jokes.punchline: When asked for specifics, he offers nothing — 'you'll know what to do' — setting up Bret to take wildly inappropriate action
jokes.explanation: This line functions as a plant for the episode's climax — 'you'll know exactly when the time is right' pays off when Bret decides to expose himself to the greeting card executive. The vagueness of the advice is the joke, and the structure of 'I don't know, I just don't give specifics, trust yourself' as cosmic guidance is satirically sharp about inspirational-speak.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Not that I did it.

jokes.text: Mel's compliment session for Bret: 'Hotter than Jemaine.' / 'You have a refined bone structure whereas Jemaine's facial features are too deep-set to be classically handsome.' / 'You're a better singer too. Yeah, Jemaine's often flat.'
jokes.setup: Mel has been enlisted to compliment Bret; she begins warmly
jokes.punchline: Each compliment for Bret becomes an increasingly specific and devastating putdown of Jemaine — the mission to boost Bret inadvertently destroys Jemaine's confidence
jokes.explanation: A masterfully constructed escalation sequence. The irony is exquisite — the plan to fix Bret's confidence ends up destroying Jemaine's. Each comparative compliment is more specific and cutting than the last: 'hotter than Jemaine' → 'too deep-set to be classically handsome' → 'Jemaine's often flat.' Jemaine's growing distress while Bret basks is perfectly played. The structure (every compliment is really an insult to someone else) is elegant and the character integration is excellent: this could only work with Mel's obsessive comparative ranking of the band members.

dialogue.line: 1980 David Bowie confirming that putting a wig on a sleeping friend isn't gay

jokes.text: After Mel leaves, Jemaine says 'What's her problem?' — completely unaware that he engineered the whole thing
jokes.setup: Mel has just delivered a barrage of compliments to Bret and comparative insults to Jemaine, at Jemaine's own request
jokes.punchline: Jemaine asks 'What's her problem?' with apparent sincerity, having apparently forgotten that he set this up
jokes.explanation: A clean irony gag. Jemaine's complete lack of self-awareness is the through-line of this sequence. The follow-up — him asking Bret if his facial features are 'too deep-set to be considered classically handsome' — is a nice additional beat of wounded vanity.

dialogue.speaker: Bowie
dialogue.line: Hey, it's 1980 David Bowie, from the music video 'Ashes to Ashes.'

jokes.text: Jemaine worriedly asks if his facial features are too deep-set to be classically handsome; Bret says 'I've got no idea'
jokes.setup: The plan was to fix Bret's confidence; it has backfired and now Jemaine has the insecurity
jokes.punchline: Jemaine earnestly asks Bret to validate his appearance; Bret offers nothing useful
jokes.explanation: A perfect role-reversal callback — Jemaine now needs the same reassurance Bret needed, completing the ironic arc of the plan. Bret's unhelpful 'I've got no idea' mirrors Jemaine's original refusal, but without even the excuse of weirdness — he just doesn't know.

dialogue.speaker: Bowie
dialogue.line: Actually, come to think of it, the same thing happened to me when I wore an eye patch. It was... oh, it was always, 'Bowie's bumped into a door.'

jokes.text: The meeting with the greeting card company — Murray explaining what a greeting card with music is to the executive who makes them
jokes.setup: Murray and the band meet David Armstrong of the greeting card company
jokes.punchline: Murray and Bret/Jemaine pepper Armstrong with increasingly basic questions about how musical greeting cards work — 'Is it a CD?' / 'Like headphones?' / 'Like a Walkman?' — forcing the man who invented them to repeatedly explain basic technology
jokes.explanation: The sustained sequence of extremely basic technology questions escalating to 'like a Walkman?' is a good cringe comedy sequence. Armstrong's mounting frustration while trying to maintain a professional meeting is well-played. The 'I guess you could say its second function is turning off' explanation is a particularly good beat.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine/Bowie
dialogue.line: Do something absolutely outrageous.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Like what?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine/Bowie
dialogue.line: Well, you'll know what to do, Bret. And you'll know exactly when the time is right.

jokes.text: Mr. Armstrong patiently explains three times how musical greeting card chips work — each time Bret/Jemaine compare it to something wrong (CD, headphones, Walkman)
jokes.setup: Mr. Armstrong is explaining the technology in greeting cards to Murray, who previously described it as 'magic'
jokes.punchline: Every explanation is met with a wrong analogy. First 'Is it a CD?' then 'Like headphones?' then 'Like a Walkman?' — with Mr. Armstrong patiently saying 'No, not like a Walkman at all' after a full explanation
jokes.explanation: A nice slow-burn running gag. The comedy of the band/manager being completely unable to grasp chip technology (which Mr. Armstrong explains with considerable effort) also pays off Murray's 'magic card' joke. Each wrong answer is slightly worse than the last: CD → headphones → Walkman is a declining arc of tech sophistication.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Well, you're looking good today, Bret. Very hot. Hotter than Jemaine.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Thank you.

jokes.text: 'Um, David, when will this meeting actually begin?' / 'Well it's... it has begun.' / 'They didn't do a roll call.'
jokes.setup: They are already in the middle of the business meeting
jokes.punchline: Jemaine doesn't recognize it as a meeting because it didn't begin with roll call — mirroring and completing Murray's opening roll-call obsession
jokes.explanation: An excellent callback with perfect structural payoff — the episode opened with Bret mocking roll call as pointless bureaucracy; now Jemaine (having absorbed Murray's meeting protocols) actually requires it to recognize a meeting as legitimate. The joke rewards viewers who remember the opening and demonstrates that the band has fully internalized Murray's absurd meeting formalism. High scores on structure and callback execution.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: You have a refined bone structure whereas Jemaine's facial features are too deep-set to be classically handsome.

jokes.text: The deal: 1¢ per card. Murray: 'Woah... we're talking serious money.' / 'Yes, we're talking serious money, and a lot of it too.'
jokes.setup: Armstrong reveals the payment rate of 1¢ per card
jokes.punchline: Murray immediately treats this as a significant financial development; Armstrong matches his enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: The 1¢ per card deal being treated as serious money is a running joke about the band's financial situation. Armstrong genuinely also calling it serious money is an interesting detail — either he too considers 50¢ significant, or he's just a good salesman.

dialogue.line: Like headphones? No, this is a computer chip... Like a Walkman. No it's not... not like a Walkman at all.

jokes.text: Murray describing Bret to the executive: 'You're the smaller one... you're into animals... you were bulimic recently... little touch of that... it's cleared up.'
jokes.setup: Armstrong asks Murray to tell him about Bret
jokes.punchline: Murray's 'pitch' of Bret to a potential business partner includes 'he's the smaller one,' a possible eating disorder, and an animal interest — while Bret protests each characterization
jokes.explanation: The convergence of the episode's running threads — Bret's size, the bulimia misdiagnosis — into a professional pitch moment is a well-executed comedy escalation. Murray's breezy 'It's cleared up' in response to a 'medical condition' he invented is very funny. Bret's repeated 'I wasn't bulimic' being steamrolled is good cringe structure. High character integration — only Murray would pitch a band this way.

dialogue.speaker: David Armstrong
dialogue.line: David Armstrong's elaborate technical explanation constantly compared to a Walkman

jokes.text: 'Were there supposed to be lightning bolts on the side of it?' / 'Yeah, looked pretty good, eh?'
jokes.setup: Murray is furious about the exposure incident
jokes.punchline: Murray's only specific inquiry is about the lightning bolt decoration — treating it as an aesthetic question rather than a legal/professional catastrophe
jokes.explanation: Tied with 'Not that I did it' as the best line in the episode. Murray's instinct to treat genital decoration as an aesthetic review item — 'Were there supposed to be lightning bolts?' — is perfectly Murray: his concerns are always procedural and visual rather than human. 'Yeah, looked pretty good, eh?' from Bret completes it with equal deadpan pride. The lightning bolt callback to Ziggy Stardust adds a layer for attentive viewers. Near-perfect economy and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Um, you were bulimic recently.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I wasn't... I wasn't bulimic.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: He's not now, he's fine now.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I'm not bulimic.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Little touch of that.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I wasn't bulimic.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It's cleared up.

jokes.text: 'David Bowie told me to do it in a dream.' / 'Oh yeah, right, Bret. David Bowie in a dream.'
jokes.setup: Murray confronts Bret about why he exposed himself
jokes.punchline: Bret's explanation — David Bowie's dream advice — is met with Murray's flat disbelief, which from the audience's perspective is funny because we've seen the dreams
jokes.explanation: The line works as a meta-joke — the audience knows Bret is telling the truth; Murray's dismissal of a literally true explanation creates dramatic irony. The 'that's typical of you' from Murray is a nice character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Whoo-hoo! Whoo! Waha!

jokes.text: Murray's self-flagellating speech about being a bad manager: 'I could have stopped you before you revealed it, or turned it into some sort of thing, like I could have said it was a cultural New Zealand greeting.'
jokes.setup: Murray blames himself for the meeting disaster
jokes.punchline: His alternative crisis management plan was to claim genital exposure as a cultural New Zealand greeting — which he could have said 'we all could have done'
jokes.explanation: Murray's instinct is not 'that was wrong' but 'I should have spun it better.' The specific alternative spin — claiming genital exhibition is a cultural New Zealand greeting, and that they could have all participated — is an inspired absurdist detail. This is extremely Murray: his management theory is essentially that any disaster can be rebranded. 'We could have all done it' is the perfect escalation of this already-absurd premise.

jokes.text: Third dream: 1986 Bowie from 'Labyrinth' — Jemaine now in a third distinct Bowie costume
jokes.setup: Bret has a third dream
jokes.punchline: Bowie appears in his most visually outrageous Labyrinth-era look — completing the trilogy of Bowie incarnations
jokes.explanation: The running gag of increasingly elaborate Bowie costumes reaches its apex. The Labyrinth look (likely including the famous tight pants) is the most visually extravagant and provides a strong visual payoff for the trilogy.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I meant on your face, Bret. On your face.

jokes.text: 'So you showed your penis to the man from the greeting-card company?' / 'That was your idea.' / 'I didn't mean something like that.'
jokes.setup: Dream Bowie confronts Bret about what he did with his advice
jokes.punchline: Even Bowie is taken aback — clarifying that 'outrageous' didn't mean this specific kind of outrageous
jokes.explanation: The confrontation between dream-guide and dreamer about the interpretation of vague advice is a great comedic premise. Bowie's 'I only meant something like...I don't know, wear makeup or...' followed by Bret: 'I was wearing makeup. I had lightning bolts on my wanger.' — this exchange is excellent. The word 'wanger' delivered deadpan is a perfect New Zealand comedy touch.

dialogue.speaker: Bowie
dialogue.line: Actually, I've totally lost confidence in my ability to help people. I'm useless.

jokes.text: 'I was wearing makeup. I had lightning bolts on my wanger.'
jokes.setup: Bowie says he only meant outrageous like wearing makeup
jokes.punchline: Bret points out he WAS wearing makeup — on his genitals — delivered with complete seriousness as if this is a reasonable clarification
jokes.explanation: One of the most quotable lines of the episode. The deadpan insistence that lightning bolt makeup on one's genitals satisfies the 'wear makeup' criterion is inspired. The New Zealand colloquial 'wanger' delivers the punchline with maximum charm. Perfect economy, perfect character — Bret's literal-minded good faith in following advice is his entire personality. This is very close to a 9-level joke.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine/Bowie
dialogue.line: I'm going to a party. I'm already 20 minutes late.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Sounds cool. Where's the party?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine/Bowie
dialogue.line: In space, Bret. In space.

jokes.text: Bowie: 'I meant on your face, Bret. On your face.'
jokes.setup: Bret has just argued that lightning bolt penile makeup counts as wearing makeup
jokes.punchline: Bowie's clarification — on your face — is delivered with the weary patience of someone who has made a catastrophic error in giving vague advice
jokes.explanation: The repetition of 'On your face' — the second one for emphasis — is the perfectly calibrated comedic beat. Bowie's tone shifts from mystical advisor to exasperated teacher. Six words, pure punch. The economy is near-perfect.

dialogue.line: Bowie going to a party 'in space'

jokes.text: Bowie admits he's totally lost confidence in his ability to help people and calls himself useless
jokes.setup: Bret asks for more advice after the disaster
jokes.punchline: Dream Bowie, having given advice that led to public indecency, declares himself useless and loses all confidence
jokes.explanation: The ironic reversal — the confidence advisor who came to boost Bret's self-esteem has now lost his own confidence — is a lovely structural echo. Bowie experiencing the same confidence crisis as Bret is the thematic joke of the episode completed. 'I might as well be' (responding to 'maybe you're just a figment of my imagination') is also a very good line.

jokes.text: 'Jemaine actually thinks that maybe you're a figment of my imagination.' / 'I might as well be, Bret. I might as well be.'
jokes.setup: Bret relays Jemaine's skepticism about the dreams
jokes.punchline: Bowie's deflated 'I might as well be' is a self-deprecating acknowledgment of his complete failure as a supernatural guide
jokes.explanation: The 'I might as well be' line does double duty — it's a meta acknowledgment that Bowie IS a figment (Jemaine in costume) and simultaneously a character beat of a defeated advisor. The line exists in two registers at once: absurdist meta-commentary and character comedy. Well-constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Do you want to borrow my jumper, Bowie? / Does the space cold make your nipples go pointy, Bowie?

jokes.text: 'I'm going to a party.' / 'Sounds cool. Where's the party?' / 'In space, Bret. In space.'
jokes.setup: Bowie announces he has to leave
jokes.punchline: The party is in space — delivered as matter-of-factly as if it's a Brooklyn loft
jokes.explanation: A beautifully absurdist setup for the final song. 'Sounds cool. Where's the party?' from Bret is the perfect straight man response — he'd go if he could. The plant for 'Bowie's in Space' is elegant. The deadpan delivery of 'In space, Bret. In space' — with the repetition for gravity — is very well-timed.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Do you use your pointy nipples / As telescopic antennae to transmit data back to Earth?

jokes.text: 'Bowie's in Space' — the full closing song, particularly 'Does the space cold make your nipples go pointy, Bowie?' and 'Do you use your pointy nipples as telescopic antennae to transmit data back to Earth?'
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine sing a song about Bowie being in space, imagining his experience
jokes.punchline: The song escalates from genuine wonder ('Isn't it cold out in space?') through concern ('Do you want to borrow my jumper?') to completely insane imagery: nipples as data-transmitting antennae, 'Mick Jaggernauts,' 'Afronauts,' 'groovatational pull,' and the two Bowies communicating with each other across space
jokes.explanation: The show's centerpiece musical achievement in this episode. The song is a masterwork of comedic escalation — it begins with the mundane concern of a New Zealand kid ('isn't it cold?', 'do you want to borrow my jumper?') and ascends through increasingly bizarre cosmic imagery. The structural genius: the song reaches 'Bowie to Bowie' (Jemaine playing both Bowie and a second Bowie communicating with the first), 'groovatational pull' as a portmanteau, 'Mick Jaggernauts' as a pun, and nipple antennae as data transmission hardware. The FOTC musical voice is at its most distinctive here: sweet, naïve, absurdist, and deeply specific. 'Bet you do, you freaky old bastard, you' is the perfect tonal pivot from wonder to affectionate irreverence. Scores at the same level as 'Bret, You Got It Going On' for different reasons — where that song is character-driven, this one is pure comedic escalation and wordplay at a very high level.

dialogue.line: Do you have one really funky sequined space suit / Or do you have several ch-changes?

jokes.text: 'Do you smoke grass out in space, Bowie? / Or do they smoke... AstroTurf?'
jokes.setup: The song speculates about Bowie's activities in space
jokes.punchline: The grass/AstroTurf pun — smoking 'AstroTurf' as the space-appropriate equivalent of marijuana
jokes.explanation: One of the sharpest wordplay gags in the episode. 'Grass' (marijuana slang) → 'AstroTurf' (synthetic grass) as the space-environment upgrade is elegant, quick, and genuinely clever. The comedic pause before 'AstroTurf' is musically timed perfectly. A great pun that earns its laugh rather than just being silly.

dialogue.line: Do you smoke grass out in space, Bowie?
Or do they smoke...
AstroTurf?

jokes.text: 'Drawn in by its groovatational pull' and 'I'm jamming out with the Mick Jaggernauts'
jokes.setup: The song describes Bowie orbiting Pluto
jokes.punchline: Two portmanteau coinages: 'groovatational' (groove + gravitational) and 'Mick Jaggernauts' (Mick Jagger + Juggernauts) delivered with total seriousness
jokes.explanation: Two exceptional portmanteaus in quick succession. 'Groovatational' — gravitational pull with groove — is a genuinely ingenious coinage that captures Bowie's aesthetic perfectly. 'Mick Jaggernauts' is even better: rock star name + mythological giant robots + space context, all in one word. These are the kinds of coinages that demonstrate real comedic wordcraft — they're funny, specific, and couldn't exist in any other song about any other subject.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I'm picking it up on my L.S.D. Screen

jokes.text: Episode coda: Bret concludes that if dream-Bowie lacks confidence, 'maybe most people aren't that confident... I'm not gonna worry about being confident.' Jemaine: 'Yeah, we don't need to worry about being confident.' Then: 'But it's not really him though. It's a dream.'
jokes.setup: The episode's emotional resolution — Bret has found peace with his body image
jokes.punchline: His reasoning is that Bowie (a figment) lacked confidence, therefore confidence is overrated. Jemaine agrees, then immediately undermines the entire logic: 'It's not really him though. It's a dream.'
jokes.explanation: A very good episode button — the resolution of Bret's arc is based on completely invalid reasoning (a dream character's confidence doesn't prove anything about real people) and Jemaine immediately and quietly notes this. The joke is that Bret's recovery is real even though its basis is logically absurd, and Jemaine's 'but it's not really him though' destroys the argument while Bret maintains his peace. Classic Conchords episodic resolution.

jokes.text: Bret says 'I'm not eating anymore. I'm a bit too fat.' — immediately after claiming his body image issues are resolved
jokes.setup: Bret has just announced he's over his body image issues
jokes.punchline: He immediately refuses Jemaine's request for his biscuit, citing being 'a bit too fat' — the resolution is immediately contradicted
jokes.explanation: A perfect ironic button — Bret's 'resolution' lasted approximately thirty seconds. The show doesn't actually resolve its characters' issues; it circles them. This is an honest and funny ending to the body image arc.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Is it a really big biscuit?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It's not a biscuit! I said no biscuit. It's not gonna be another kind, is it?

jokes.text: Murray's 'guess what's in my hand' game: guesses are 'a biscuit' and 'another kind of biscuit' and 'a really big biscuit'
jokes.setup: Murray arrives with exciting news and wants to build suspense with a guessing game
jokes.punchline: The band's guesses all escalate within the biscuit category; Murray's 'It's not gonna be another kind, is it?' / 'Is it a really big biscuit?' is the perfect escalation of the gag
jokes.explanation: A beautifully absurd guessing-game sequence. The failure mode of the guessing game — Jemaine guessing 'another kind of biscuit' after 'a biscuit' was explicitly ruled out — and Bret's escalation to 'a really big biscuit' captures the deadpan absurdism of the band perfectly. Murray's exasperation: 'You've ruined this game. I'll just show you.' is the right button.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Can I get a copy of that, Murray?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No no, 'cause that would cost more than 50¢, and then we'd... we'd be down, wouldn't we?

jokes.text: The greeting card deal: half a million cards speculated, but only 50 are being made. 'We've got 50¢, so how much are your coffees?' / '60¢.' — they can't afford coffee
jokes.setup: Murray announced 'serious money' from the greeting card deal
jokes.punchline: Total earnings: 50¢. Coffee costs 60¢. They are 10¢ short of being able to afford coffee from their creative achievement.
jokes.explanation: The arithmetic is the joke and it's executed perfectly. The cascade: 1¢ per card → half a million cards (serious money!) → only 50 cards printed → 50¢ total → coffee costs 60¢ → they cannot afford coffee. The deflation from financial fantasy to inability to buy a 60¢ coffee is an elegant structural joke about the band's situation. 'We'd be down, wouldn't we? The whole thing would be a waste' from Murray treating 50¢ as a meaningful budget is characteristically deadpan.

dialogue.line: Final reprise of 'Bowie's in Space' with vocal flourishes

jokes.text: Jemaine can't get a copy of the card because 'that would cost more than 50¢, and then we'd be down, wouldn't we? The whole thing would be a waste.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine asks if he can get a copy of the greeting card featuring their song
jokes.punchline: Murray does the math: one card costs more than their entire earnings from the deal. The whole deal's profit margin cannot absorb one copy for the artist.
jokes.explanation: The perfectly logical extension of the 50¢ joke. Murray's concern that buying one copy of the card 'would be a waste' — meaning they'd net less than zero — is the show's commentary on the band's career trajectory. The financial math is actually correct, which makes it funnier.

tmdb_overview: After a photo session, Bret develops body image issues and gets some dream advice from his idol, David Bowie. Jemaine plots to cheer him up and Murray tries to get one of the band's tunes used for a musical greeting card.
